_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning how to use our knowledge of base words, prefixes and suffixes — and the rules for adding endings to words — to read and spell more words.

_source.w: We are learning how to use our knowledge of base words, prefixes and suffixes — and the rules for adding endings to words — to read and spell more words.
y: Morphological knowledge is one of the most powerful tools for expanding our vocabulary.
Understanding how words are built helps us decode unfamiliar words and spell them correctly.
These patterns work across thousands of words, so learning them pays off enormously.
eg: We identify the base word, prefix and suffix in words like 'unhappiness' (un + happy + ness).
We learn the rules for adding suffixes — like doubling the consonant in 'running' or dropping the 'e' in 'making'.
We use prefixes to work out the meaning of unfamiliar words — 'dis' means 'not' or 'opposite'.
We build word families from a base word, adding different prefixes and suffixes.
We apply suffix rules when spelling — knowing when to change 'y' to 'i' before adding '-ed'.
We use morphemic knowledge to read a new word by recognising its parts.
